title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Public Works Committee approved several infrastructure items including a resolution supporting state transportation funding, awarded a $126,900 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel, and directed staff to create enforcement language for sewer discharge violations with fines up to $100/day. The committee also approved construction agreements for Military Road utilities and inspection services for upcoming road projects.

agenda.item: Acknowledge April 2026 water and sewer permits
Review 2025 Water Utility PSC annual report
Recycled asphalt base course production contract

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a Local Road Improvement Grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Scofield Avenue and Community Center Drive, covering over 50% of the estimated $1.5 million project cost. This 2029 project may be moved up to 2028. Michael noted approximately $9 million in grant funding has been secured over the past 4-5 years. Discussion included Weston Avenue repairs by Integrity Grading and Excavating for road settlement over sewer trenches, and Scofield Avenue repairs by HOS Construction for pavement marking and manhole issues.

Staff reported road limits were removed April 29th along with Marathon County. Spring activities included mailbox repairs, sign repairs, curb line sweeping, and hot mix patching. Storm damage cleanup was conducted off East Nick with tree damage but no home damage. Culverts failed on Jones Street requiring installation of a third 18-inch pipe. Salt supply is adequate with 1,500 tons ordered and 200 tons arriving this month.

Josh reported significant clear water infiltration into the sanitary sewer system - April showed 37 million gallons pumped into the distribution system but 53-54 million gallons treated, indicating approximately 15 million gallons of clear water infiltration. The INI study with in-pipe technologies will help identify problem areas. The Kathleen lift station was identified as having issues with sump pump discharge from nearby homes. Well 2 rehab with Carrie started today.

Michael presented the annual Public Service Commission report showing net operating income of $482,911 in 2025, consistent with $490,000 in 2024. Water loss is at 6%. The utility value increased to nearly $34 million with $6.7 million in infrastructure placed in service. The utility serves 6,100 customers total - 5,250 in Weston, 830 in Rothschild, and 5 others. Motion to acknowledge passed unanimously.

Michael presented a League of Wisconsin Municipalities sample resolution supporting long-term transportation funding solutions. He noted the village has decreased public works budgets in recent years and Wisconsin remains affordable compared to neighboring states for automobile ownership. Motion to adopt passed unanimously.

Michael explained the Ryan Street site stockpiles recycled concrete and asphalt for crushing into base course. King Gravel submitted the low bid at $4.23/ton for an estimated 30,000 tons totaling $126,900. This compares favorably to City of Wausau paying $28/cubic yard versus Weston's $17.11/cubic yard, saving approximately $130,000 across three 2026 projects. Motion to award to King Gravel passed unanimously.

Staff sought guidance on enforcement of sewer discharge violations, particularly sump pump connections to sanitary sewers. Current code allows fines of $100-$1,000 per day. Committee discussed distinguishing between unknowing and willful violations. Committee member Zagami suggested waiting for the INI study results, then sending violation letters with 30-60 days to correct. Committee directed staff to create enforcement language with fines not to exceed $100/day for first offenses. Motion passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Water and Sewer Connection Agreement - Radkey Avenue
discussions.body: Michael presented an agreement for a duplex at 2230 Radkey Avenue to temporarily connect via long lateral until Radkey Avenue reconstruction extends mains through Outlot 1. Committee directed adding language that reconstruction occur no later than 2029. Motion to recommend approval with the 2029 deadline passed unanimously.

The committee approved an intergovernmental agreement with Village of Rothschild for Military Road construction. Weston's share for sanitary sewer ($6,156) and water main ($88,704) relocation totals $94,860. Steam Construction is the contractor. Motion to recommend approval passed unanimously.

Michael presented inspection service proposals for two projects. For Alderson/Jelinek intersection roundabout: MSA at $37,315 (recommended) vs Clark Dietz at $48,910. For Bladel Avenue: MSA at $70,400 vs Clark Dietz at $73,730. Michael recommended Clark Dietz for Bladel due to having full-time experienced staff versus MSA's summer intern. Motion to approve MSA for roundabout and Clark Dietz for Bladel passed unanimously.

actionItems: Staff to add date language (no later than 2029) to Radkey Avenue connection agreement
Staff to create enforcement language for sewer discharge violations with fines up to $100/day
King Gravel awarded recycled asphalt crushing contract at $4.23/ton ($126,900 estimated)
Village Board to adopt Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding
Village Board to approve Military Road construction agreement with Rothschild ($94,860 Weston share)
MSA to provide construction inspection for Alderson/Jelinek roundabout project ($37,315)
Clark Dietz to provide construction inspection for Bladel Avenue project ($73,730)
Special assessment meeting scheduled for May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM for Bladel Avenue project
Norcon to begin concrete repairs on Scofield Avenue eastbound lanes starting Wednesday/Thursday
Staff to provide cost analysis of clear water infiltration for next meeting
Staff to provide street lighting update at future meeting

title: Tourism Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Tourism Commission held its reorganization meeting, electing new officers and approving a $5,000 grant for the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. The commission also heard that a competing balloon event in Marshfield was canceled, potentially increasing attendance at the Weston event.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
New business - Grant request: Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Remarks from chair and future items

discussions.body: Trustee Erling was nominated and elected as Chair with unanimous approval. Cushing was nominated for Vice Chair by a commission member, seconded by Jackson, and approved unanimously. Renee was nominated and elected as Secretary, also unanimously approved.
The minutes from the April 21st meeting were approved. The motion was seconded by Renee and passed unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Grant Request: Taste and Glow Balloonfest
discussions.body: Brandon Jill from Taste and Glow Balloonfest (3308 Green Pastures Lane) presented the grant request. He reported attendance of 11,000-12,000 last year and announced that a competing Marshfield balloon event was canceled due to permit issues, making theirs the sole hot air balloon event in the area. A commission member noted the application showed 20 fewer rooms than last year and requested better tracking of hotel room usage. The commission approved $5,000 (same as last year) unanimously, with the expectation of receiving detailed hotel usage data in the post-event report.

Renee reported on attending the Alice in Dairyland dinner at Red Rock Orchard in Edgar. She described how the candidates toured Marathon County locations including Mullen's Cheese, noting this was the first time Marathon County hosted the event since the 1970s. The winner, Anastasia, had three bachelor's degrees and the candidates were grateful for the community's hospitality.

actionItems: Approved $5,000 grant to Taste and Glow Balloonfest for their annual event
Taste and Glow Balloonfest to provide hotel room usage data in their post-event report
Next meeting scheduled for July 20th at 4:00 PM

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved a site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Drive, and adopted a new salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection areas. Both items passed unanimously with all present commissioners voting in favor.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Open public comment period for items not on agenda
Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive
Creation of salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
Planning and development department monthly project update

discussions.item: Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive. The building was rotated to address challenges with a gas pipeline easement running through the property. Chief Jeremy explained the building has capacity for 50-60 officers versus current staffing of 40 officers plus 4 clerical staff. An alternate storage shed was discussed as a potential add-on depending on bid costs. Questions were raised about future expansion capability, with confirmation the building could expand to the north. The landscaping plan meets current relaxed code requirements. Motion to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees with conditions 2-5 passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
discussions.body: Staff proposed a program with two annual inspections per facility - one warm weather inspection focused on structural integrity and one winter operational inspection. A standardized inspection worksheet similar to DOT's was developed. Staff identified one existing facility near the village well on Sternberg that never went through the wellhead protection conditional use process. Commissioners agreed staff should reach out to that facility to bring it into compliance. Motion to approve the program with reporting back to both Plan Commission and Public Works Committee passed unanimously.
Motion to approve as presented was made and seconded. Passed unanimously without discussion.

discussions.item: April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
discussions.body: Approved unanimously without discussion.
Approved unanimously without discussion.

discussions.item: Planning and development department monthly update
discussions.body: Staff announced the first meeting of the joint Plan Commission/Village Board steering committee for the comprehensive plan update is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m. Staff will send email confirmations and calendar invites.

publicComment: Jim Pensel of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke via online connection. He requested the Plan Commission urge staff to waive excessive landscaping requirements for the new police department project to save taxpayer money, similar to accommodations made for the Greenheck Turner building. He also requested annual reporting on the salt shed inspection program to ensure complaints don't get lost.
actionItems: Site plan for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive recommended for approval to Board of Trustees with conditions 2-5
Salt storage shed inspection program adopted for wellhead protection district with twice-annual inspections
Staff to report inspection program results to both Plan Commission and Public Works Committee annually
Staff to reach out to existing salt storage facility on Sternberg that lacks conditional use approval
First comprehensive plan steering committee meeting scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 p.m.
Staff to send email and calendar invites for steering committee meeting
Electric vehicle charging station regulations to be addressed in fall agenda

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an educational orientation meeting for new board members, featuring presentations from the Health Department, Veteran Services, and Aging Disability Resource Center. The committee approved April 1st meeting minutes and received comprehensive overviews of departmental missions, budgets, and programs, with no major policy decisions made.

agenda.item: Call to order and remote participation confirmation
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operational functions required by statute, ordinance, resolution or policy
Review of standing committee ordinance section 2.04 - missions, duties, responsibilities

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavelli made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Vice Chair Rosenberg. The motion carried unanimously with all in favor.

discussions.item: Review of standing committee ordinance and jurisdiction
discussions.body: Administrator Lance Leonard explained the committee's structure, noting jurisdiction over the Health Department, Social Services, Veteran Services, North Central Health Care, and Aging Disability Resource Center. He outlined statutory responsibilities and explained that subordinate bodies like the Social Services Board and Veteran Service Commission provide input but don't directly supervise department heads. Chair Boots noted the committee will focus on developing a new strategic plan by December.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented the department's mission to prevent disease, promote health, and protect against environmental hazards. She highlighted over 1,200 licenses overseen, a new press release on tick prevention with free tick kits available in public health vending machines, the Nurse Family Partnership program with first graduates at age two, and a new homeless system response position. The department has approximately 37.6 FTE with about half of its $5 million budget from tax levy. Supervisor Kremes asked about pandemic preparedness; Suginaka confirmed plans are in place including recent measles response training.

Administrator Leonard presented on behalf of absent Director Christa, describing the department's divisions: child welfare (protective services and youth justice), children's long-term support, economic support, and child support. With nearly 140 FTE, they are the second largest department. Their $22 million budget includes about $7 million in tax levy. Leonard highlighted the successful Family Keys program addressing housing instability, noting many clients had never had bank accounts or formal leases before the program.

Veteran Service Officer Aaron Galindo reported his office increased from 2.75 to 3.0 FTE in 2025, with customer interactions jumping from 5,900 to 8,149. He noted $44 million annually flows to local veterans, with $2.6 million in retroactive payments in 2025. Key initiatives include housing stability work with Center for Veterans Issues and suicide prevention efforts - Marathon County had 30 suicides in 2025, seven of which were veterans. Supervisor Kremes asked about regional coordination; Galindo explained state statute requires each county to have its own VSO but offices collaborate regularly.

Executive Director Mike Ray explained the ADRC serves four counties (Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, Wood) as an integrated organization providing aging and disability services. Approximately 11% of their budget comes from county levy. He noted significant challenges with the nutrition program (Meals on Wheels) as federal funding has been flat while food costs increased 40-45% over four years. The organization has about 75 staff and relies on 385-400 volunteers. Vice Chair Rosenberg praised the culturally appropriate nutrition program at the Mung American Center.

actionItems: April 1st meeting minutes approved
North Central Health Care to be scheduled for separate full presentation
ADRC Executive Director to present annual report with 2025 data to full board
Chair Boots to bring strategic plan discussion to future meetings
Next meeting corrected to Tuesday, June 3rd at 3:00 PM (not May 29th as originally stated)

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increased park and recreation impact fees, passed several ordinances including regulations for mobile food vendors and e-bikes, and discussed ongoing challenges with the farmers market relocation to Kennedy Park. The board also adjourned the Board of Review to June 15, 2026.

agenda.item: Board of Review called to order and adjourned to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees meeting called to order
Public hearing on park and recreation impact fees (Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015)
Acknowledgment of reports from boards, committees, and commissions with chair updates
Consent agenda including vouchers and license renewals
Ordinances including mobile food vendor, nuisances, and e-bikes
Discussion on farmers market location at Kennedy Park

discussions.body: Staff presented a proposal to increase park and recreation impact fees from $300 per single family unit to $761, from $250 to $563 for duplexes, and from $250 to $495 for multifamily units, based on a 2022 public facilities needs assessment. Jim Pitel spoke during public comment questioning the lack of specific cost data to justify the fees. Trustee Clark supported the increase, noting population growth requires more park infrastructure. Ordinance 26-010 was approved with motion by Lopesrl and second by Zagami, passing unanimously. Resolution 2026-015 was also approved unanimously with motion by Hermling and second by Clark.

Chief Finkey explained that Ordinance 26-011 creates a reciprocal inspection agreement between Safer and municipalities including Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, and others. Once a food truck is inspected by any participating department, a sticker indicates compliance for all communities, eliminating redundant inspections. Motion by Lopesrl, second by Zagami, passed unanimously.

The board debated amendments to the remote meeting attendance policy. Original language included requirements about health, safety, and welfare reasons for remote attendance. Several trustees argued this was too restrictive and inconsistent with prior discussion. Trustee Beck moved to amend by striking sentences requiring specific justification, seconded by Clark. The amendment passed with two no votes from Hermling and Lopesrl. The original motion as amended then passed with the same two dissenting votes.

The president appointed Stephanie Daniels (currently on Finance and HR) and Golden Mua to the CLIPS committee. Motion by Hermling, second by Lopesrl, passed unanimously. Parks, Finance/HR, and CLIPS still have openings. The board agreed to include applicant resumes in future packets.

Extended discussion on challenges with the farmers market's new location at Kennedy Park after losing the Camp Phillips/Schofield Avenue site to development. Trustee Beck raised safety concerns about cars parking on grass along Alderson Street. Renee explained history: the market grew from 25 stalls at the original location to about 53 at Kennedy Park, and vendors now can keep vehicles with them. The board discussed but took no action, acknowledging the situation is evolving and may require finding a new location if issues persist.

Public Works Director Michael reported on speed study data showing the 85th percentile speed was 45 mph, which could support keeping the 45 mph limit. A second study is needed but was delayed due to construction. Trustee Zagami noted a resident complaint that the speed limit has been inconsistent with ordinance for over two months. Staff will bring this back to the board, possibly at the joint meeting.

Michael addressed Jim Pitel's public comment about the unfilled engineering technician position, explaining the position requires a specific skill set that is scarce in central Wisconsin. Even consulting firms are losing employees to relocation. The board approved two engineering consultant contracts for projects starting in June due to the vacancy.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of 2nd Street spoke during public comment criticizing the hiring of outside engineering consultants for $108,000 when an engineering technician position remains unfilled. He questioned administrator accountability and criticized the lack of transparency in committee appointments, noting Lisa Beck was introduced at a Safer meeting before being formally appointed. He also criticized the absence of detailed Kennedy Park information in the packet. Pitel later spoke during the impact fee public hearing questioning the lack of specific operational cost data to justify the fee increases.
actionItems: Board of Review adjourned to June 15, 2026
Park and recreation impact fees increased per Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015
Ordinance 26-008 approved for mobile food vendor reciprocal inspection program
Ordinance 26-009 approved amending committee structure
Ordinance 26-011 approved amending nuisances chapter
Ordinance 26-012 approved regulating e-bikes and e-motos on sidewalks
Resolution 2026-014 adopted supporting sustainable state transportation funding
Remote meeting attendance policy amended to remove specific justification requirements
Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua appointed to CLIPS committee
Secondhand dealer license approved for Schofield Coin and Hobby LLC contingent on background check and occupancy permit
Compliance assurance plan approved
First comprehensive plan steering committee meeting scheduled for Monday, June 1 at 6:00 pm
Staff to bring back Weston Avenue speed limit issue to board
Committee applicant resumes to be included in future board packets
Intergovernmental service agreement to be developed for Ringle building inspections

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board of Supervisors held an adjourned organizational meeting where they approved committee appointments, elected Supervisors Hapa and Arstead to the executive committee, and unanimously approved a resolution reducing landfill tipping fees for severe weather debris following the recent tornado and flooding disaster affecting Ringle and other communities. The board also approved several consent agenda items and budget resolutions related to out-of-home placement and social services.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Reading of the notice and roll call
Election of at-large executive committee members
Resolution approving out-of-home placement reserve increase (D15)
Resolution to amend social services budget (D16)
Resolution approving modification to landfill tipping fees (E17)
Vote on suspension of rules for tipping fee resolution

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs outlined goals for committee appointments including increasing diversity of perspectives, developing new supervisors as leaders, and accommodating supervisor preferences. Vice Chair Boots assisted in developing the appointments. The motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Crims, was carried but not unanimous. Over 34% of the board is in its first or second term.

discussions.item: Election of At-Large Executive Committee Members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated for two at-large executive committee positions: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Vice Chair Boots). Supervisor Rosenberg explained she nominated Supervisor Leur because she 'advocates for our county's smallest children and our oldest elders.' Paper ballots were distributed and Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the executive committee.

discussions.item: Landfill Tipping Fee Modification for Disaster Debris
discussions.body: Administrator Leonard and Solid Waste Director Hagen Busher presented a proposal to reduce tipping fees for debris from the recent tornado and flooding. The state waived $13.10 of fees under the emergency declaration, and Ringle waived their $2.33 fee. The county proposed reducing fees to $36.94 per ton (covering costs only) rather than the standard $65 gate rate. Two residents, Jessica Toasty and Matt Haybeck, were granted special dispensation to speak, asking the county to waive fees entirely. Toasty noted that the community has been 'very self-reliant' with businesses donating up to $40,000 in cleanup expenses. The board voted unanimously to suspend rules to consider the resolution directly, then unanimously approved Resolution 26-26 modifying the tipping fees through June 17th.
Items C10 through C14A were approved unanimously on a motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck.
Resolution 24-26 was approved on a motion by Supervisor Morash, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.
Resolution 25-26 was approved on a motion by Supervisor P, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

publicComment: Two residents were granted special permission to address the board regarding the landfill tipping fee resolution. Jessica Toasty asked the county to waive fees entirely for six months, noting that the state, DNR, and Ringle have waived their fees and that private businesses have donated significant resources for cleanup. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of residents whose homes must be demolished, asking for relief for underinsured homeowners and requesting more time for the fee waiver since some insurance adjusters haven't yet arrived.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved as presented by Chair Gibbs and Vice Chair Boots
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to at-large executive committee positions
Resolution 26-26 approved reducing landfill tipping fees to $36.94 per ton for certified disaster debris through June 17th
Administrator Leonard to work with Community Foundation on mechanism for reimbursing uninsured debris disposal costs
Solid Waste Management Board to develop long-term policy on handling disaster-related fee reductions within six months
Resolution 24-26 approved for out-of-home placement reserve increase
Resolution 25-26 approved amending social services budget

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee met on May 5, 2026, approving two rezoning petitions unanimously and voting to forward a modified fee waiver resolution to the county board that will only waive reconnect fees for septic systems damaged by the April 17, 2026 tornado. The committee also received educational presentations from Solid Waste, Parks Recreation and Forestry, and Conservation Planning and Zoning departments.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public hearing - Tim Veland rezoning petition (urban residential/general agriculture to rural residential, Town of Green Valley)
Public hearing - Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture, Town of McMillan)
Consideration of fee waiver for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Department overview - Parks, Recreation and Forestry (Tom Lovelin)
Department overview - Conservation Planning and Zoning (Lori Misskowski)

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes with Supervisor Drebeck seconding. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Tim Veland rezoning petition - Town of Green Valley
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning from urban residential and general agriculture to rural residential for a land division. The Town of Green Valley recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact, conclusions of law, and town approval. Supervisor Kurt seconded. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition - Town of McMillan
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the petition to rezone from Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture. Supervisor Leur asked about the conservancy nature and potential loss of natural areas. Pogle explained the property contains a home and horse riding stable, and rezoning makes the home a conforming use rather than non-conforming. Town of McMillan recommended approval April 20, 2026. Supervisor Sefeld moved to approve; Supervisor Drew seconded. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Fee waiver for April 17, 2026 severe weather
discussions.body: Director Lori Misskowski presented a proposal to waive county zoning and septic fees for properties damaged by the April 17 tornado affecting towns of Ringle, Easton, villages of Kronenwetter and Weston. Significant debate ensued about precedent-setting, fairness to other disaster victims, and costs. Supervisor Drebeck proposed an amendment to only waive reconnect fees ($160) for septic systems, not other zoning or new system fees. The amendment was seconded by Supervisor Kurt and passed. The amended resolution was then approved to forward to county board, though not unanimously.

David Hagenbusher presented Solid Waste overview covering landfill operations, RNG partnership with Vidi Energy, leachate treatment requirements by 2032, and capital expenses. Tom Lovelin presented Parks, Recreation and Forestry overview, noting his upcoming retirement June 1st after 35 years and successor Joe Tucker. Lori Misskowski began Conservation Planning and Zoning overview.

actionItems: Forward amended fee waiver resolution to county board - only waiving reconnect fees ($160) for septic systems damaged by April 17 tornado
Forward James Offer/David Tract rezoning petition (urban residential/general agriculture to rural residential) to county board for approval
Forward Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture) to county board for approval
CPZ staff to track costs and requests related to tornado fee waivers for future policy development
Town of Green Valley and Town of McMillan recommended to update comprehensive plans to reflect zoning changes if applicable

title: Parks Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Parks Committee approved installation of new kayak launch signs for the Plover River, deferred discussion on allowing dogs at the aquatic center until next month when the committee chair can be present, and received updates on Kennedy Park fundraising and the Yellow Banks disc golf course logging project. The meeting also included public comment expressing opposition to dogs at the pool and criticism of Kennedy Park financial transparency.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Yellow Banks disc golf course update
Dog Day at the aquatic center discussion
Graffiti on the Oaklair River Trail discussion

discussions.body: Director Sean Osterbrink presented a timeline of the Kennedy Park master plan project from its 2023 approval to the May 9th ribbon cutting. Committee member Scott asked about timelines for fundraising, and Osterbrink clarified that the project could take up to 10 years with no firm deadline. The Friends of Kennedy Park are currently fundraising for $117,000 for field lights and have approximately $84-85,000 at the community foundation. Osterbrink noted the Friends group plans a public check presentation at their June tournament. The acting chair defended the volunteer group, stating they deserve 'a little bit of grace' and aren't withholding information.

discussions.item: Yellow Banks Disc Golf Course Update
discussions.body: Staff presented photos and a PDF showing the location of fallen trees removed from the river and the installation of wood mats to protect gas pipelines during the logging operation. Dan explained the mats were installed over two pipeline crossings to allow heavy equipment access. ATC (now TC Energy) representatives were on site to monitor the work. The village now has extra mats that may be used on another trail project.

discussions.item: Dog Day at the Aquatic Center
discussions.body: The committee deferred this item to the June meeting because Committee Chair Katrina, who requested the item, was absent due to an unforeseen issue. One public commenter, Andy from 6504 [street name inaudible], stated strong opposition: 'There should not be animals allowed into a public pool. Period... We have rivers. We have lakes. That's where they belong.' The acting chair noted Andy's objection would be recorded.

discussions.item: Graffiti on the Oaklair River Trail
discussions.body: Staff discussed challenges removing graffiti from concrete and memorial benches along the trail, noting their best tool (a sand blaster/pressure washer) cannot access the trail due to bridge weight limits and terrain. Options discussed included anti-vandalism coatings, sealer applications, and potentially organizing volunteer workdays. Committee member Laro suggested community members might volunteer to help with maintenance. No formal action was taken; staff will research options.

Dan Wierzba from Marathon County (2625 Northwestern Avenue) presented version 7 of signs for Plover River kayak launches. Three of eight launches are in Weston (Highway J, Ross Avenue, and Yellow Banks). Signs will include maps, QR codes linking to river gauge data, and location-specific history sections. The Highway J launch may be closed all season due to bridge replacement. The committee approved installation of the signs unanimously to be forwarded to the Village Board.

Staff asked about committee interest in food allergy awareness signage for playgrounds, noting an individual offered to provide signs encouraging keeping food away from play equipment. The committee agreed to bring this back for future discussion. Staff also announced the Mock Miller Park master plan process will begin with a kickoff meeting with Rettler, and the final bike and pedestrian plan open house is June 11th from 5-7pm.

publicComment: Two public comments were received. Andy (6504 [street inaudible]) spoke in person opposing dogs at the aquatic center, stating animals don't belong in public pools. Jim Pensan (5002 [street] Weston), speaking online under the name 'Vern M', criticized the 4 p.m. meeting time as discouraging public participation and questioned Kennedy Park financial reporting, calling the $8,700 figure 'too coincidental' and asking about the $10,000 grant and Glow event funds, suggesting the Friends of Kennedy Park are being secretive about finances.
actionItems: Approved minutes from April 27, 2026 meeting
Deferred Dog Day at Aquatic Center discussion to June meeting
Approved installation of Plover River kayak launch signs at Weston locations, forwarded to Village Board
Staff to research graffiti removal options and potential anti-vandalism coatings for trail amenities
Staff to bring food allergy awareness signage proposal back for future discussion
Mock Miller Park master plan kickoff meeting with Rettler scheduled for next Tuesday
Next meeting scheduled for June 22, 2026

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Finance and Human Resources Committee approved previous meeting minutes and acknowledged April budget and financial reports. The meeting featured an educational presentation from Municipal Clerk Pam on clerk's office operations, elections, licensing, and tax collection, with discussion about pet licensing fee increases and newsletter costs.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting
TIFF one and TIFF two detail reports for April
Educational presentation - Municipal Clerk's office and budget
Staff remarks and farmers market discussion

discussions.item: Approval of minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting
discussions.body: Motion to approve made by Ernling, seconded by Daniels. Motion carried with no discussion.

discussions.item: TIFF one and TIFF two detail reports
discussions.body: Jessica provided an update on TIFF bond payoff procedures, anticipating October/November timeline. The CDA will need to pass a resolution to call the bonds, which are due October 1st. Jessica confirmed the village will be able to pay off the debt. A committee member asked about final numbers and was assured they still look good.
Motion to acknowledge the April budget status report, TIFF reports, and legal details report made by Daniels, seconded by Soaper. Motion carried.

Clerk Pam presented on the clerk's office operations, staffing (now 2 full-time and 1 part-time employee, down from 4 full-time previously), elections administration, licensing, and tax collection. She noted 84% of tax collection ($22 million) is handled at the clerk's office counter. Discussion occurred about pet licensing fee increases from $10 to $20 for neutered dogs, which was prompted by Marathon County raising fees to municipalities. Committee members questioned the $30,000 annual newsletter cost, with staff noting previous attempts to eliminate mailing were reversed due to resident complaints.

Renee reported the farmers market has been going well, with staff arriving at 6 AM on Saturdays to help vendors. The market is full with food trucks, coffee vendors, craft people, and some produce vendors with greenhouses. One committee member who spoke with vendors reported they were very happy with the setup and location. Discussion occurred about parking concerns on Alderson and Newport streets, with staff confirming parking is restricted to the west side of Alderson only.

Evan announced he is stepping down from the committee as he and his wife are moving away from Weston, though staying in the metro area. This was his last meeting. The chair noted there are now open spots on this committee and encouraged members to reach out to interested citizens.

actionItems: CDA resolution needed to call TIFF bonds, anticipated this summer
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, June 15th at 4:30 PM
Committee seeking citizen volunteers to fill open committee positions

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee met on May 4th, taking action on several ordinance amendments and property matters. Key decisions included recommending approval of nuisance code amendments, an e-bike ordinance with added emoto restrictions, mobile food vendor fire inspection requirements, address corrections, and a raze order for an abandoned property at 4104 Shore Avenue.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Mobile food vendors fire inspection amendment

discussions.body: Jim Senalt addressed the committee with concerns about the new farmers market location at Aquatic Park, stating it was 'a terrible execution' and predicting the grassy areas would be destroyed. He raised issues about vehicles parking on grass potentially violating village code, ADA-compliant handicap parking being placed on grass rather than asphalt, questioned the need for new fire inspections on food trucks, and noted that some code enforcement cases have been pending for years without visible progress.

Code Enforcement Officer Audrey reported that 3020 Mountain View Avenue will be removed from the code enforcement roll after the property sale was completed and new owner submitted repair permits. Chief Hunt reported the police department had 2,394 calls in March (77 per day), 410 traffic stops, and 281 citations issued. The department has hired its 40th officer, with the 38th starting last week, 39th starting in May, and 40th around January. Two SROs work with zoning violations during summer for all three municipalities. Motion to acknowledge reports 5, 6, and 7 passed unanimously.

The committee discussed final amendments to Chapter 50, including previously discussed changes to tree limb height requirements (14 and 10 feet) over streets and walkways, with added language requiring compliance with jurisdiction authority. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey, to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees passed unanimously.

Extended discussion occurred regarding the model e-bike ordinance. Chief Hunt strongly advocated for prohibiting emotos (electric dirt bikes) from roadways and sidewalks, comparing them to unlicensed dirt bikes. Committee member Mike Compi raised concerns about electric scooters used by middle school students. The committee decided to add emotos to the prohibited list for roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property while leaving officer discretion for kids' play scooters. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Chwanka, to recommend approval with emoto restrictions passed unanimously.

Committee member Tolson presented research showing Weston's 12-hour parking restriction (4am-4pm) is significantly longer than surrounding municipalities which typically have 4-5 hour windows. Chief Hunt opposed eliminating overnight parking entirely, preferring uniformity with other municipalities. Public Works Director Mike suggested potentially eliminating the broad restriction and instead signing the 6-8 specific problem areas. The committee decided to table the item until July, directing Mike to identify specific exception areas and develop logistics for a more targeted approach.

Mount Olive Church requested a crosswalk between their property and DC Everest school parking lot. Staff estimated a basic crosswalk would cost approximately $2,000-3,000, while a lighted system would be $25,000-30,000. Committee members were reluctant to set precedent for paying for requested crosswalks. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Chwanka, passed unanimously to recommend board approval contingent on Mount Olive covering the installation cost. Mike will contact Mount Olive to determine their willingness to pay.

The committee discussed adding fire inspection requirements for mobile food vendors through a multi-jurisdictional agreement with Wisconsin Rapids, Marshfield, Stevens Point, Plover, Rib Mountain, and SAFER. Fire Marshal Marty already inspected one vendor that failed due to improper LP piping and unsecured propane containers. The program allows any participating department to inspect trucks, with a sticker indicating compliance good for all jurisdictions. Motion by Chwanka, seconded by Campkey, to approve Ordinance 26-8 passed unanimously.

Staff presented three address corrections needed for E911 compliance: 5915 Delicowski Street (duplex needing separate addresses), 3913-3917 Ross Avenue (out of sequence addressing), and 2211 Radkey Avenue (new duplex on private drive). Chief Hunt added that Weston Villa Apartments on Delicowski has each of its eight units with separate addresses rather than unit numbers, requiring review. Motion passed unanimously to recommend address corrections to the Board of Trustees.

Staff requested authorization to begin raze proceedings for an abandoned structure at 4104 Shore Avenue, abandoned since 2010-2011. A previous attempt to use the structure for fire department training was abandoned due to unsafe conditions. The current owners, who are up to date on taxes but operating a small farm, had previously housed chickens in the structure. Demolition costs will be placed on the tax roll. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey, passed unanimously to recommend the Board authorize the raze order.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke during public comment, criticizing the new farmers market location at Aquatic Park as 'a terrible execution' that would destroy grassy areas. He raised concerns about vehicles parking on grass violating village code, questioned ADA compliance of handicap parking on grass, asked about the origin of new food truck fire inspection requirements, noted ambiguity in mobile food vendor regulations on public property, and criticized the lack of accountability on long-standing code enforcement cases.
actionItems: Recommend Chapter 50 nuisance amendments to Board of Trustees for May agenda
Recommend e-bike ordinance with emoto restrictions to Board of Trustees
Mike to develop specific problem areas for overnight parking restrictions for July meeting
Mike to contact Mount Olive Church regarding willingness to pay for crosswalk installation
Recommend Ordinance 26-8 (mobile food vendor fire inspections) to Board of Trustees
Recommend address corrections for 5915 Delicowski, 3913-3917 Ross Avenue, and 2211 Radkey Avenue to Board of Trustees
Staff to investigate Weston Villa Apartments addressing issue raised by Chief Hunt
Recommend authorization for raze order at 4104 Shore Avenue to Board of Trustees
Staff to investigate property at Sternberg and South Timber with furniture and dumpster in yard
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, July 7th at 5:00 PM

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

agenda.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling (Action Requested)
Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code (Action Requested)

discussions.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Thomas Jefferson Elementary as part of the Excellence in Action program. No additional details or presenter information were provided in the agenda.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in an estimated 5 minutes. Action was requested for this policy update.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on Employee Handbook Updates for 2026-27
Board was expected to vote on the Preliminary 2026-27 Budget recommendation

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on approval of the Consent Agenda including appointments, separations, leaves of absence, retirements, meeting minutes, payment of bills, and donations
Action was requested for the 2026-27 Budget Reconciliation Plan
Board was expected to vote on 2026-27 Teacher Contract Approvals
Action was requested for Various Group Wage/Salary Increase of 2.63% for non-teaching staff
Board was expected to vote on WAMCS Charter School Contract Renewal for 2026-2031

The commission also received updates on lead service line replacements and wastewater projects.

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Week of the Young Child Proclamation
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.

Special assessments levied for 2025 Street Construction Projects
